speaker: interviewer
text: Briefly how you came to Australia and then talk about life here.

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, yes, well, by 1949, '48, it was-- it was evident that we have to find a more permanent solution. Belgium is not a solution. Besides, I didn't want to stay in Europe. So I had two alternatives-- Israel or somewhere else. The remaining part of-- big part of the remaining part of my family went to Israel by that time-- two uncles and their children. There were four children involved-- so four girls. And I wrote to them, and, you know, I asked them, you know, whether should come. And they had been-- that was in 1948. That was the formation of Israel. They had been going through very difficult times. And they didn't encourage me a great deal at that time. And I-- I somewhat felt that they are so preoccupied with their own-- own worries that it wouldn't be fair if I-- if I, you know, insisted on that they should be enthusiastic about my coming out there. And rightly or wrongly, I have decided that I will take up this other opportunity, which happened through an acquaintance of mine. We had a cousin here who sent me a landing permit to Australia. I had a trade. I was young, so there was a desirable element to come into Australia. And in 1949, I went to Marseilles. And from there, we embarked and we came to Australia by a ship which is called which was an Italian troop carrier during the war-- a very primitive ship, which meanwhile sank, I believe. It was so old. And the journey lasted, well in excess of six weeks when we arrived to Australia to Fremantle, I think, on the 15th of May, if I remember well.

speaker: interviewer
text: All right, and what did you do when you first arrived here?

speaker: interviewee
text: When I came to Australia I-- I worked as a toolmaker. I immediately started to work. In '49, there was an ugly coal strike. There was still the shortages of-- after the war-- shortages prevailing. And I was lucky. I got a job at ACI, the glass factory, where I worked as a toolmaker. I got 8 pounds and 14 schillings. That was my-- my first award wage, quite a good one. Toolmakers earned good, good money at that time. And most importantly, we worked right through the coal strike because we had our own emergency plans.

speaker: interviewer
text: And I worked as a toolmaker, And in-- on the 1st of September, 1951-- you wouldn't believe it-- but I had the good fortune of winning the lottery-- six of us. They called it the six happy toolmakers. And we won 6,000 pounds, which meant 1,000 pounds for each of us. And I mean, I earned eight pounds a week, so that gives you a-- you know, what-- what this money meant at that time. It was two years salary, right? It was a lot of money. And-- and-- I-- I then I left toolmaking. And I, as a toolmaker still, I got involved with the son of the person who was my sponsor, right, who had a knitting mill-- it was a knitting machinery type of thing. And he asked me one day, well, why don't you come and work for me? You organize outside, and I knit inside. And that's what we did. I was reasonably organized outside.

speaker: interviewee
text: By 1956, I became a one-third partner-- his-- his mother, himself, and I. It was a very modest set up still. In 1960, Ernie died, and then I decided to-- to, you know, start my present activity. And since I'm a glutton for punishment, I'm still in it.

speaker: interviewer
text: And your family-- where did they begin?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I met my wife-- I met my wife in '51. My wife is-- is a non-Jewish Irish girl. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] I-- I met her, and I married her in 1956, five years later. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] In 1957, my double cousin came out from Hungary with his parents. And they have two daughters here and grandchildren by now. In 1963, my son was born. and-- and [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I'm very, very grateful to Australia for-- for, you know, providing me the-- the type of opportunities what I-- I grabbed with two hands. I'm particularly grateful to Australia that, you know, I-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] you know, it encouraged me to become an Australian and yet allowed me to stay Jewish. My Jewishness, strangely, blossomed later on. When I married my wife earlier in those years, my-- I-- it has always been dormant, very strong. But it has not been as dominant as it is today, strangely. In those younger early years of-- of reestablishment, I had other priorities. But as-- as, you know, time goes on, I realized more and more where I belonged and the stories I told you determined my belongings.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK, thank you very much. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] All right, Mr. Angles, can you introduce the two people in the middle, first of all?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, well, this family photo was taken of my father's family in 1924, the year I was born. This is my grandfather, [? Ariesh ?] Angles. This is my grandmother, [? Mena ?] Angles, nee [? Mena ?] [? Offenberger. ?]

speaker: interviewer
text: All right, and where is your father?

speaker: interviewee
text: My father is here. He was 39, 40 years of age on this-- on this-- on this occasion.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK, and one other relative who had particular significance in the story-- the cousin that you took out of the ghetto--

speaker: interviewee
text: The-- the father of my cousin, yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: The father. The father.

speaker: interviewee
text: That is [? Izador ?] [? Basczi. ?] This-- this man here, who incidentally came out to Australia, joining his daughter and myself and who died here in, unfortunately, in 1962.

speaker: interviewer
text: Right, and this is the man who did not respond properly to your acting skills?

speaker: interviewee
text: Correct. [LAUGHS] [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: All right, and who are these people?

speaker: interviewee
text: Now these people are my mother's family. My grandfather, Joseph Angles-- Joseph Nathan, who died in December 1929. That's my grandmother, [? Luska ?] Nathan, born [? Luska ?] [? Kline. ?] My mother is the-- the eldest daughter. They have 1, 2 more daughters and 1, 2, 3 more sons. One person-- a rather important person related to my wartime stories-- the baker, Ernie, is not here. So they had therefore 1, 2, 3, 4, 5, 6, 7, plus the baker, eight children. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: And who is this person?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, if you can believe it, this is me at the age of 18 in 1942 on location of matriculation ceremony. As you can see, I'm dressed in the Hungarian what we called [? botchki ?] outfit, which is a typical Hungarian nationalistic dress. That was the-- that was a must of the times.

speaker: interviewer
text: And was it a big ceremony?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, it was a big event. The ceremony, itself, wasn't so big. But it was a huge event. It was a-- a-- a big stone fell off our-- our heart, having concluded our-- our-- our high-- high-- high-- high school studies. And it was a tremendous relief for me, I remember. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: OK, Mr. Angles, I recognize you. Can you describe the others?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, Danny, this has been taken in 1943, I would say, during my toolmaking apprentice time. It was taken on an island, which was on the Danube, which we called Mosquito Island. [NON-ENGLISH SPEECH] I remember. This is myself. This is my mother. Incidentally, this is the only photo I have of my mother, and that was preserved by my friend, George [? Falurie, ?] who is also here in Australia. And that is his-- that is his mother. And as I said, that was taken on a Sunday outing when we have been out on the Mosquito Island. And I'm very grateful to George that he preserved this for me.

speaker: interviewer
text: Good. Thanks. OK, Mr. Angles, when was this taken?

speaker: interviewee
text: That was taken in, I would say, in 1963, a few months after Mark was born. Of course, when Mark entered our life, that was a-- a new era completely. He has completely taken over, emotionally, our attention. And that was the time of my-- my life when-- when, apart from strongly trying to establish-- re-establish myself here, I really enjoyed my early role-- and I'm sure my wife could say the same-- as young parents. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] This photo, here, marks the occasion of Mark's bar mitzvah. Mark was 14 by the time he had his bar mitzvah. I'm not sure why they-- why it took us so long, but one day I just woke up, and I decided that Mark must have his bar mitzvah. And fortunately Mark, by that time, got very strongly involved with [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Jewish studies in-- very modestly. I must not like to exaggerate the depth of his knowledge but sufficiently to become a bar mitzvah boy. And that was one of the happiest time-- times of my life. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] Danny, you must know that my son, early after high school studies, worked for me for one year. However, fortunately for him, he saw the daylight, and he went back to studies. He became a microbiologist. And what you see here is the graduation ceremony involving obtaining his papers. [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: OK, Mr. Angles.

speaker: interviewee
text: Now, in the chronological order, this is the last photo, which shows Mark's wedding to [? Allison. ?] This is a family photo. This occasion took place last year. So it's fairly recent, and it has very pleasant, very fond memories for me. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: All right Mr. Angles, can you introduce your family, please, everybody?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, coming from right to left-- this is my only daughter-in-law, [? Allison, ?] and my favorite one. This is my only and favorite son, Mark. And this is my only and favorite wife, Nancy.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you. All right, I have just two questions. The first one is for Mrs. Angles, which is, when you-- well, when you met your husband in-- in the early years of your marriage, were you aware of the kind of place and the kind of events he was coming from?

speaker: interviewee
text: No.

speaker: interviewer
text: No?

speaker: interviewee
text: It wasn't mentioned whatsoever.

speaker: interviewer
text: And since then, has he talked about it?

speaker: interviewee
text: Not very much until this year. The 50th anniversary brought it all out.

speaker: interviewer
text: Really?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK, well there's a lot of it on tape now.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was that the same for you, Mark?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, yes. We had discussed a few things earlier, but not-- not-- I-- this year's been a real eye-opener for me as well.

speaker: interviewer
text: Really? Right.

speaker: interviewee
text: Just to hear the whole story in one hit is pretty good.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yeah, well tell me about it. The one thing I have to ask is did you hear the one where he scales the burning building with the ammunition dump on the top-- on a length of rope?

speaker: interviewee
text: No I haven't heard that one.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK, well, I highly recommend that part of the videotape. And thank you all for coming.

speaker: interviewee
text: Thank you.

speaker: interviewer
text: And thank you Mr. Angles, again.
